# Transcrição: 02-How to Get Advisors With Little to No Money Out of Pocket

- **Fonte:** 02-How to Get Advisors With Little to No Money Out of Pocket
- **Duração:** 03:03
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

In this lesson, we're going to briefly talk about hiring advisors and professionals that you'll need in order to complete your deal. These are your lawyers, your CPAs, and so on. However, we're going to go about this in a way that protects you from having to pay these advisors with your own cash. The goal is to hire these advisors and pay them using the business's money when the deal gets done. That's where contingent fee advisors come in. The reason we call them contingent fee advisors is because you're only going to pay these advisors if the deal gets done. And obviously, there's some risk to these people because there's no guarantee that the deal is 100% going to get done. But usually by the time you need them, the deal is already pretty close to the finish line. In any case, before you reach out to these professionals, a couple things you need to know. First, you need to sign a letter of intent or an LLI with a seller. The letter of intent proves that you're looking to purchase the business and you've made proposals to the seller. Someone who's being paid on a contingent basis will want to know you've got a deal lined up and that the deal is on its way to being completed. Next, you'll need to have proof of funding. This is where the lessons in the module come in. You show them the deal on paper and then they'll tell you how much funding they can provide. Once you have those two things, you can start looking for advisors when you need them. As I mentioned, for these advisors, there's added risk because their payment is contingent on the deal getting completed. It may be more difficult to find people willing to help. Business of the lawyers and accountants you talk to will be familiar with this model, so it's a good place to start. There are even some agencies that specialize in these types of payment methods. Another reminder, you're not the only one paying the advisor. Ultimately, once the deal gets done, the business will fund the fees you agreed to pay. If for some reason the deal falls through, then the advisors aren't allowed anything. On top of not getting a deal done, you definitely want to avoid upsetting these advisors. Those relationships are very important to maintain and grow as you look at more deals if your deal doesn't close. So if the deal does fall through, one option might be to give that person a first right of refusal for the next deal you do. That means they'll have the first right to enter into your business transaction before anybody else. And if the deal falls through, the accountant and the lawyer that you work with on a contingent fee basis will usually make higher adjustments in their fee structures. It's not a huge deal since the business is paying for it, but something to be aware of. That's everything I'm going to cover on the contingent fee advisors. It was a short lesson, but it's important to have a baseline understanding.

## [03:01 - 03:03]
